reasoning:
The baseline 1-month yield (reference June 8) stands at 4.28% (using June 6 data). The 1-month yield closely mirrors the average expected effective federal funds rate (EFFR) over the subsequent 30 days. 
1. June 15 (p=0.70): A 1-month bill issued on June 15 spans until July 15, covering only the June 18 FOMC meeting. At 4.28% (below the 4.33% EFFR), the current yield prices in roughly a 33% chance of a June rate cut. By June 15, market expectations typically crystallize. With consensus heavily favoring a 'Hold' for June, expectations will likely revert toward 0% chance of a cut, pushing the yield back up to 4.33% (i.e., an increase). The FRED linear model corroborates this, projecting 4.31% with a 0.69 probability of increase.
2. July 8 (p=0.45): A July 8 bill covers the July 30 FOMC meeting. Even if the Fed holds in June, if they strongly signal a July cut (prob > 75%), the expected rate average will dip below 4.28%. It will take a scenario where both June and July meetings feature 'Holds' or low-confidence cuts to stay above 4.28%. 
3. Sept 6 (p=0.25) & Dec 5 (p=0.15): Markets price in 2 to 3 quarter-point rate cuts before year-end, which would pull the 1-month yield structurally down toward 4.08% or 3.83%. It would require the Fed to make zero cuts to remain above 4.28% — a hawkish tail-risk scenario given current macroeconomic projections. Consistent with forecasting norms, probabilities drift toward (but do not cross) 0.5 to model wider event variances across longer horizons.
